content: A string of length $L$ and force constant $k$ is stretched to obtain extension $l$. It is further stretched to obtain extension $l_1$. The work done in second stretching is
explanation:
Work done in stretching a string to obtain an extension l,

$$ W_1=\frac{1}{2} k l^2 $$

Similarly, work done in stretching a string to obtain an extension $l_1$ is,

$$ W_2=\frac{1}{2} k l_1^2 $$

∴ Work done in second case, $W$

$$ =W_2-W_1=\frac{1}{2} k\left(l_1^2-l^2\right) $$

content: A block of mass 10 kg slides down a rough slope which is inclined at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ to the horizontal. The coefficient of sliding friction is 0.30 . When the block has slide 5 m , the work done on the block by the force of friction is nearly
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text {As, } F=\mu m g \cos \theta \\ & \text { or } \quad F=0.30 \times 10 \times 10 \cos 45^{\circ} \\ & \text { or } \quad F=\frac{30}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} W & =F \times s \times \cos 180^{\circ}=-\frac{30}{\sqrt{2}} \times 5 \\ & =-\frac{150}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}}=-75 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned} $$

content: A body is projected with a speed $u \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ at an angle $\beta$ with the horizontal. The kinetic energy at the highest point is $(3 / 4)$ th of the initial kinetic energy. The value of $\beta$ is
explanation:
As the vertical component of the velocity at highest point is zero.

$$ u_y=0, u_x=u \cos \beta $$

The kinetic energy at the highest point will be equal to $\frac{1}{2} m(u \cos \beta)^2$

The initial kinetic energy $=\frac{1}{2} m u^2$

$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{2} m(u \cos \beta)^2=\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{1}{2} m u^2$

$\Rightarrow \quad \cos \beta=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Rightarrow \beta=30^{\circ}$

content: A force of $ F=0.5 \mathrm{~N} $ is applied on lower block as shown in figure. The work done by lower block on upper block for a displacement of 3 m of the upper block with respect to ground is (Take, $ g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2} $ )
explanation:
To determine the work done by the lower block on the upper block, follow these steps:

Calculate Maximum Possible Acceleration:

The maximum acceleration the upper block (1 kg) can experience without sliding off is due to static friction:

$ a_{\text{max}} = \mu g = 1 \, \text{m/s}^2 $

where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction and $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity.

Calculate Common Acceleration:

The applied force is distributed over the total mass (3 kg) of the system, so the acceleration $a$ is:

$ a = \frac{F}{m_{\text{total}}} = \frac{0.5}{3} = \frac{0.5}{3} \, \text{m/s}^2 $

Check for Relative Motion:

Since $ a < a_{\text{max}} $, the blocks will move together without slipping.

Calculate the Force of Friction:

The force of friction acting from the lower block to the upper block is:

$ f = m_{\text{upper}} \cdot a = 1 \cdot \frac{0.5}{3} = \frac{1}{6} \, \text{N} $

Calculate the Work Done by Friction:

The work done by this friction force over a displacement of 3 meters is:

$ W = f \times s = \frac{1}{6} \times 3 = 0.5 \, \text{J} $

Thus, the work done by the lower block on the upper block for a displacement of 3 meters is $0.5 \, \text{J}$.

content: A pendulum of mass 1 kg and length $ l=1 \mathrm{~m} $ is released from rest at angle $ \theta=60^{\circ} $. The power delivered by all the forces acting on the bob at angle $ \theta=30^{\circ} $ will be (Take, $ g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2} $ )
explanation:
Power of tension $ =0 $

Power of $ m g=(m g)(v) \cos 60^{\circ} $

Here, $ \quad v=\sqrt{2 g h} $

and $ \quad h=l\left(\cos 30^{\circ}-\cos 60^{\circ}\right)=0.36 \mathrm{~m} $

$ v=\sqrt{2 \times 10 \times 0.36} $

$ =2.68 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $

Power $ =(m g)(v) \cos 60^{\circ} $

$ \begin{array}{l} =(1 \times 10)(268) \times \frac{1}{2} \\ =1.34 \times 10=13.4 \mathrm{~W} \end{array} $

content: An ideal massless spring $ S $ can be compressed 1 m by a force of 100 N in equilibrium. The same spring is placed at the bottom of a frictionless inclined plane inclined at $ 30^{\circ} $ to the horizontal. A 10 kg block $ M $ is released from rest at the top of the incline and is brought to rest momentarily after compressing the spring by 2 m . If $ g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2} $, what is the speed of mass just before it touches the spring?
explanation:
To find the speed of the mass just before it touches the spring, we begin by determining the spring constant, $ k $, using Hooke's Law, where the force $ F $ is proportional to the compression $ x $:

$ F = kx $

Given that a force of 100 N compresses the spring by 1 m, the spring constant $ k $ is calculated as:

$ k = \frac{F}{x} = \frac{100 \, \text{N}}{1 \, \text{m}} = 100 \, \text{N/m} $

Next, we apply the principle of energy conservation. The initial potential and kinetic energy will equal the final potential energy stored in the compressed spring. The relevant energy conservation equation is:

$ \frac{1}{2} mv^2 + mgh = \frac{1}{2} kx_{\text{max}}^2 $

where:

$ m $ is the mass of the block (10 kg),

$ g $ is the acceleration due to gravity (10 m/s$^2$),

$ h $ is the vertical height change, which is the same as the compression in this scenario ($x_{\text{max}} = 2 \, \text{m}$),

$ v $ is the speed just before touching the spring,

$ x_{\text{max}} $ is the maximum compression of the spring (2 m).

Rearranging the equation for $ v $ gives:

$ v = \sqrt{\frac{k x_{\text{max}}^2}{m} - 2gh} $

Substituting the known values:

$ v = \sqrt{\frac{(100 \, \text{N/m})(2 \, \text{m})^2}{10 \, \text{kg}} - 2(10 \, \text{m/s}^2)\left(\frac{2}{2}\right)} $

This simplifies to:

$ v = \sqrt{\frac{(100)(4)}{10} - 40} $

$ v = \sqrt{40 - 20} = \sqrt{20} \, \text{m/s} $

Thus, the speed of the mass just before it touches the spring is $ \sqrt{20} \, \text{m/s} $.

content: The potential energy for a force field $$\mathbf{F}$$ is given by $$u(x, y)=\cos (x+y)$$. The force acting on a particle at position given by co-ordinates $$(0, \pi / 6)$$ is
explanation:
To find the force acting on a particle in a force field given by a potential energy function, we can use the relationship between force and potential energy. The force is the negative gradient of the potential energy function:

$$\mathbf{F} = -\nabla u(x, y)$$

Given the potential energy function:

$$u(x, y) = \cos(x + y)$$

The gradient operator $$\nabla$$ in two dimensions is given by:

$$\nabla = \left(\frac{\partial}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\right)$$

To find the force, we first need to compute the partial derivatives of $$u(x, y)$$ with respect to $$x$$ and $$y$$.

Calculate the partial derivative of $$u(x, y)$$ with respect to $$x$$:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\cos(x + y) = -\sin(x + y)$$

Now, calculate the partial derivative of $$u(x, y)$$ with respect to $$y$$:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\cos(x + y) = -\sin(x + y)$$

So the gradient of $$u(x, y)$$ is:

$$\nabla u(x, y) = \left(-\sin(x + y), -\sin(x + y)\right)$$

The force $$\mathbf{F}$$ is the negative of this gradient:

$$\mathbf{F} = -\nabla u(x, y) = \left(\sin(x + y), \sin(x + y)\right)$$

We need to evaluate this at the coordinates $$(0, \pi / 6)$$:

$$ (x, y) = (0, \pi / 6) $$

Substitute these values into the sine function:

$$ \sin(0 + \pi / 6) = \sin(\pi / 6) = \frac{1}{2} $$

Thus, the force $$\mathbf{F}$$ at $$(0, \pi / 6)$$ is:

$$\mathbf{F} = \left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

In vector notation, this can be written as:

$$ \mathbf{F} = \frac{1}{2} \hat{\mathbf{i}} + \frac{1}{2} \hat{\mathbf{j}} $$

This matches with Option B:

$$\frac{1}{2}(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}})$$

content: A machine gun fires 300 bullets per min if the mass of each bullet is 10 g and the velocity of the bullets is 600 ms$$-$$1, the power (in kW) of the gun is
explanation:
work done by the gun = total kinetic energy of the bullets

$$ = n{1 \over 2}m{v^2} = 300 \times {1 \over 2} \times 10 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times {(600)^2}$$

$$ = 150 \times 10 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times 600 \times 600$$

Power of gun $$ = {{work\,done} \over {Time\,taken}}$$

$$ = {{150 \times 10 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 600 \times 600} \over {60\,s}}$$

= 9 kW ($$\because$$ 1 minute = 60 seconds)

content: A force F = a + bx acts on a particle in the x-direction where a and b are constants. The work done by this force during a displacement from x = 0 to x = d is
explanation:
Work done by the particle in X-direction is given by

dW = F dx = (a + bx) dx

Total work done in displacement from x = 0 to x = d will be

$$W = \int {dW = \int\limits_0^d {(a + bx)dx} } $$

$$ = \left[ {ax + {{b{x^2}} \over 2}} \right]_0^d$$

$$ \Rightarrow W = \left( {a + {{bd} \over 2}} \right)d$$

content:
The bob of a pendulum of length 2 m lies at P, when it reaches Q, it losses 10% of its total energy due to air resistance.

The velocity of bob at Q is
explanation:
Kinetic energy at Q = 90% of potential energy at P

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over 2}m{v^2} = {{90} \over {100}} \times mgh$$

$$ \Rightarrow v = \sqrt {1.8\,gh} \Rightarrow v = \sqrt {1.8 \times 10 \times 2} $$

$$ \Rightarrow v = 6$$ m/s

content: A ball of mass 0.5 kg is thrown up with initial speed 16 ms$$-$$1 and reaches maximum height of 9 m. How much energy is dissipated by air drag acting on the ball during the ascent?
explanation:
Given, initial speed of ball, v = 16 ms$$-$$1

Maximum height, h = 9 m

Mass of ball, m = 0.5 kg

Energy supplied to ball while throwing up,

$${E_1} = {1 \over 2}m{v^2} = {1 \over 2} \times 0.5 \times 16 \times 16 = 64$$ J

Energy stored when ball reaches the maximum height,

E2 = mgh = 0.5 $$\times$$ 9.8 $$\times$$ 9 = 44.1 J

Energy dissipated by air drag acting on the ball during the ascent,

E = E1 = E2 = 64 $$-$$ 44.1 = 19.9 J

content: A time dependent force F = (8t) acts on a particle of mass 2 kg. If the particle starts from rest, the work done by the force during the first 1s will be
explanation:
Given, force, F = (8t) N, Mass, m = 2 kg

From Newton's second law,

Force = Rate of change of momentum

$$F = {{dp} \over {dt}}$$

i.e. dp = Fdt

$$\therefore$$, $$p = \int {dp = \int_0^1 {F\,.\,dt = \int_0^1 {8t\,.\,dt} } } $$

$$ = 8\left[ {{{{t^2}} \over 2}} \right]_0^1 = 8\left( {{1 \over 2} - 0} \right)$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ p = 4 kg ms$$-$$1

Change in kinetic energy, $$\Delta K = {{{p^2}} \over {2m}} = {{{{(4)}^2}} \over {2 \times 2}} = 4$$ J

From work-energy theorem,

Work done = Change in kinetic energy

So, work done by the force during the first 1s,

W = $$\Delta$$K = 4J

content: The work done by a force acting on a body is as shown in the following graph. The total work done in covering an initial distance of 40 m is
explanation:
Work done, W = area under F-s graph

$$\therefore$$ Total work done is covering an initial distance of 40 m,

W = area of trapezium ABCD + area of trapezium CEFD

$$ = {1 \over 2} \times 20(20 + 30) + {1 \over 2} \times 10 \times (20 + 40) = 800$$ J

[$$\because$$ Area of trapezium = $${1 \over 2}$$ $$\times$$ height $$\times$$ sum of parallel sides]

$$\Rightarrow$$ W = 800 J
